content.full_text:
REED & DAVIDSON
ATTORNEYS AT LAW

DANA W. REED
CARY DAVIDSON
BRADLEY W. HERTZ
DARRY L. R. WOLD
OF COUNSEL

888 WEST SIXTH STREET, 12™ FLOOR
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90017
TELEPHONE (213) 362-9238
FACSIMILE (213) 623-1692

ORANGE COUNTY OFFICE
3181 AIRWAY AVENUE, SUITE M-1
COSTA MESA, CALIFORNIA 92626
TELEPHONE (714) 641-688
FACSIMILE (714) 546-1603

October 16, 1990

Ms. Jeanne Pritchard
Division Chief
Technical Assistance and Analysis Division
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Government Code § 87103.5 Income from
Retail Sales Exception to Conflict of Interest
Provisions of the Political Reform Act

Dear Ms. Pritchard:

This letter requests advice from the Fair Political Practices Commission, pursuant to Government Code § 83114(b).¹ I am requesting informal assistance as a person with a duty to advise other persons relating to their actions under the Political Reform Act (2 California Code of Regulations, § 18329(c)).

Facts

A city council candidate owns and controls a weekly newspaper. A corporation places advertisements for itself, unrelated to the election, in the newspaper at a cost in excess of $250. The candidate wins the election and becomes a city council member. Within 12 months of the advertising purchase, a matter comes before the city council which has a material financial effect on the corporation, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally.

If the advertising purchase renders the corporation a "source of income" to the council member (§ 87103(c)), then the council member would have a financial interest in a decision which has a foreseeable, material financial effect on the corporation (§ 87103). Because the council member may not be involved in decisions in which he knows he has a financial interest, he would ordinarily have to disqualify himself from the decision.

¹All statutory references are to the California Government Code, unless otherwise specified.

Ms. Jeanne Pritchard
Fair Political Practices Commission
October 16, 1990
Page 2


     However, if the advertising purchase could fit into the
"income from retail sales" exception (§ 87103.5), then the
corporation would not be a source of income and the council
member would not have to disqualify himself from the decision.

Questions Presented

1.  Is the corporation considered a "source of income" to the
council member by virtue of the advertising purchase
(§ 87103(c))?

2.  If the answer to Question 1 is yes, can the "income from
retail sales" exception be applied so the corporation might
not be a "source of income" (§ 87103.5)?

Discussion

     In the Commission's Smith letter (I-88-456, 1/25/89), the
"income from retail sales" exception was applied to a flower shop
which sold flowers to the public. A newspaper sells advertising
space and subscriptions to the public and appears to be a
business entity engaged in retail sales. Accordingly, if the
newspaper's retail customers constitute a significant segment of
the public generally, and the amount of income received by the
newspaper from the corporation is not distinguishable from the
amount of income received from its other retail customers, the
corporation's advertising purchase should not be a source of
income to the council member.

     If you have any questions or require any additional
information, please feel free to contact me.

                                   Very truly yours,

                                   [signature]
                                   Bradley W. Hertz

BH00928.1


[illegible]

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a corporation that purchases advertisements in a city council member's newspaper at a cost exceeding $250 constitutes a "source of income" requiring the council member to disqualify himself from decisions affecting that corporation, and whether the "income from retail sales" exception under Government Code § 87103.5 would apply to exclude the newspaper advertising from conflict of interest rules.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: This is an incoming request letter from an attorney seeking FPPC advice, not an FPPC response letter. The document includes a discussion section that appears to be the attorney's preliminary analysis rather than FPPC's formal conclusion. No formal CONCLUSION section from FPPC is present. Document references Smith letter (I-88-455) for precedent on retail sales exception.

2. If the answer to Question 1 is yes, can the "income from retail sales" exception be applied so the corporation might not be a "source of income" (§ 87103.5)?

embedding.summary: An attorney requests informal FPPC advice regarding whether a corporation purchasing advertising in a newspaper owned by a city council member creates a conflict of interest requiring disqualification, and whether the retail sales exception applies to exclude such advertising income from conflict of interest provisions.
